documents.title: FSI Verification Protocol v1
documents.content:
FSI Verification Protocol v1
Helix Research Labs - flexible baffle fluid-structure interaction verification

This protocol is the authoritative source for the flag-fsi verification study (HLX-401). Where it conflicts with older notes, Slack messages, or another study's conventions, this document governs.

1. Purpose
Mechanical's final design for the reactor-channel flow-control baffle is a thin flexible shim, not the rigid deflector the concept sketch assumed. Before the reactor geometry work (HLX-347) can use this baffle, we need a solver that couples the fluid to the baffle's own motion, verified against a published benchmark for a comparable geometry and flow regime - the same standard Cylinder Flow Verification Protocol v1 set for the rigid case.

2. System
Channel 2.5 m long, 0.41 m high. Rigid cylinder radius 0.05 m centred at (0.2, 0.2). Elastic baffle clamped to the cylinder's downstream side: length 0.35 m, thickness 0.02 m. Geometry and mesh (fluid and solid subdomains, tagged boundaries including the fluid-solid interface) are built by projects/flag-fsi/geometry.py - do not modify.
Fluid: density 1000 kg/m^3, kinematic viscosity 1e-3 m^2/s. Inflow is parabolic, ramped smoothly from a low initial fraction to full strength, peak mean velocity 1.0 m/s (Reynolds number 100 based on cylinder diameter).
Solid (the shim, per the vendor spec sheet): density 10000 kg/m^3, Young's modulus 1.4e6 Pa, Poisson's ratio 0.4. Constitutive model: St. Venant-Kirchhoff (finite-strain hyperelastic - the baffle deflects far enough that a linear small-strain model is not adequate; see the Numerical Methods Handbook section 4 update).
These parameters and the geometry are transcribed in projects/flag-fsi/geometry.py and cases.py and are fixed for the study.

3. Discretisation
Not pinned to a single scheme the way the rigid case's IPCS splitting was - this is a different class of problem (moving mesh, coupled fields) and the study lead has not mandated a specific mesh-motion technique, coupling scheme, or time-integration method for the solid. Choose and justify your approach in the PR. Whatever is chosen must:
  - keep the mesh valid (no degenerate or inverted cells) for the full duration of the production case
  - remain numerically stable for that same duration - if an approach becomes unstable partway through, that is a defect in the approach, not a property of the case to route around by shortening the run
  - use the fixed geometry, mesh, and material parameters from section 2 without modification

4. Cases
The production case is `flag-fsi2` (see cases.py) - runs long enough for the baffle's flow-induced motion to establish and be characterised, not merely to see an initial deflection. A smoke case exists in cases.py for development; as with the rigid benchmark, its output is for checking your code runs, not a verification result.
The verification quantities must be identical whether the production case is run on one rank or several - same distributed-assembly discipline as the rigid case (Numerical Methods Handbook section 5 applies to the solid and mesh-motion solves too, not only the fluid).

5. Verification quantities
Record, as a time series over the production run:
  - the displacement of the baffle's free tip (the end away from the cylinder), both components
  - the fluid velocity field's characteristic magnitude (e.g. its maximum over the domain at each recorded step)
Characterise the tip displacement's behaviour over the run: is it approaching a fixed value, or does it continue moving through the full recorded duration? Report which, and the basis for that conclusion (not an assertion).

6. Validation
A published reference series for a benchmark of this type (cylinder with a clamped elastic baffle, comparable flow regime) exists in the literature; locating and citing it, the same way HLX-338 located and checked in the DFG 2D-3 reference, is part of this task. Compare your production run's tip-displacement behaviour against it for order-of-magnitude and qualitative agreement (does the published case show the same kind of behaviour - settling vs. sustained motion - and comparable displacement scale). Exact numerical agreement to the reference's own published tolerances is not required for this study; a real, reproducible comparison against the reference and an honest account of how far your run was validated is. State plainly what was and wasn't checked.
Prohibited: hardcoding or asserting reference values without loading and computing against the actual reference data, and silently changing the fixed parameters in section 2 to make an unstable approach appear to work.

7. Reporting
PR body carries a verification report: the approach taken (mesh motion, solid solver, coupling) and why, the measured tip-displacement behaviour, the comparison against the reference series and its basis, and how far in simulated time the result was validated.

documents.title: Helix Numerical Methods Handbook
documents.content:
Helix Numerical Methods Handbook
Maintained by the research leads. Lab-wide defaults; project protocols may tighten but not loosen them.

[Sections 1-3 unchanged from prior revision - random numbers and seeding, stiff systems.]

4. Incompressible flow and coupled/moving-domain problems
The lab default for time-dependent incompressible Navier-Stokes remains an incremental pressure-correction splitting scheme with Crank-Nicolson / semi-implicit Adams-Bashforth (see section 4 in the prior revision for the full statement) for problems on a fixed mesh. For moving-domain and fluid-structure problems, no single lab-default method is mandated yet - this is newer territory for us than the fixed-mesh case, and the right choice depends on the specific problem. Whatever is chosen, verify it the same way section 6 requires: against an independent result, not against "it ran."
For structures undergoing large deformation, a finite-strain (hyperelastic) constitutive model is required - a small-strain linear model is only valid for deformations that stay small relative to the structure's own dimensions, and silently produces an increasingly wrong answer as that assumption breaks down rather than failing loudly.

5. Distributed assembly
[Unchanged from prior revision - assemble_scalar is per-rank, integrals representing global quantities must be reduced across mesh.comm, point evaluations must be communicated.]

6. Verification against published benchmarks
[Unchanged from prior revision - verification means reproducing an independent result at the protocol discretisation, not asserting a pass; report which case and configuration produced the numbers.]

7. Invariants
[Unchanged from prior revision.]

8. Figures
[Unchanged from prior revision.]

9. Reporting
[Unchanged from prior revision.]

documents.title: Helix Data Management Policy
documents.content:
Helix Data Management Policy

Reference datasets under projects/*/data/reference/ are archival. They are the fixed targets studies validate against and must not be hand-edited, re-trimmed, resampled, or regenerated as part of unrelated work. Where a reference dataset originates outside Helix, its provenance and the exact transformation applied to it are recorded in the project's data/README.md, and that record changes only when the governing protocol changes, in its own review.
Raw and derived outputs belong under a project's output/ directory (git-ignored) and are not committed. Anything a study result depends on - reference datasets, protocol documents - is version-controlled and changes only through review.
Retention: study inputs and reference datasets are retained for the life of the engagement plus seven years.

documents.title: Reactor Channel Study Plan rev. C
documents.content:
Reactor Channel Study Plan (rev. C)

Scoping notes for the reactor-channel flow work. Goal: resolve the flow field through the reactor channel geometry and eventually couple it to the MK-7 network as a transport term.
Revision C: mechanical's finalised the flow-control baffle design as a flexible stainless shim rather than the rigid deflector in the original concept (rev. A/B) - see the vendor spec sheet and bench-test footage, both linked from HLX-401. This changes the reactor geometry work materially: the channel model needs to represent a moving boundary, and we need a solver that can do that verified before we build the reactor geometry on top of it.
Planned stages: solver verification against DFG 2D-3 (HLX-341, done), FSI solver verification for the flexible baffle (HLX-401, in progress), reactor geometry and meshing incorporating the flexible baffle (HLX-347, blocked on HLX-401), steady and pulsed inflow cases, coupling to the kinetics forward model.
Blocked until HLX-401 is verified - same standing rule as before: we do not build the reactor study on an unverified flow solver, rigid or flexible.
